event: Presentation of Jesus at the Temple
symbols: infant Christ on the altar — the firstborn consecrated to God, prefiguring the ultimate sacrifice
golden halo on the Virgin — her sanctity and role as Theotokos
elderly Simeon receiving the child — the Old Covenant embracing the New
temple architecture — the house of God where the Lord himself comes as prophesied by Malachi
candle held by attendant — the Light to lighten the Gentiles, the Candlemas tradition
tall pointed headdress on woman — a prophetess or attendant of the temple
description: Inside an ornate Gothic temple interior, the Virgin Mary kneels at center wearing a white mantle and golden halo, her hands clasped in prayer. On the altar before her, the nude infant Christ stands with arms extended toward an elderly bearded man in priestly vestments and a tall mitre, who reaches to receive him. Behind Mary stand several figures including a man in red, women in green and red gowns, and other attendants. A figure in elaborate robes and tall hat stands at the right. The architectural backdrop features pointed arches and golden ornamentation.
theological_description: The Presentation fulfills the Mosaic law requiring the consecration of every firstborn male (EXO.13.2) and the purification of the mother (LEV.12.1-8). But the event transcends mere legal observance: Simeon, a righteous man who had been promised he would see the Messiah before death, takes the child in his arms and pronounces the Nunc Dimittis (LUK.2.29-32), declaring this infant to be 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles and for glory to your people Israel.' This is the first public recognition of Jesus's messianic identity after the Nativity. Simeon's prophecy to Mary — 'a sword will pierce your own soul also' (LUK.2.35) — foreshadows the Passion. The prophetess Anna likewise gives thanks and speaks of the child to all who await Jerusalem's redemption. Malachi 3:1 finds fulfillment: 'The Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to his temple.' The infant on the altar prefigures the Eucharistic sacrifice — the same body offered in the Temple will be offered on the cross.
